anchor.snippet: a point z 0 is a pole of a function f if it is a zero of the function 1/ f and 1/ f is holomorphic
A function f is meromorphic in an open set U if for every point z of U there is a neighborhood of z in which at least one of f and 1/ f is holomorphic.
If f is meromorphic in U , then a zero of f is a pole of 1/ f , and a pole of f is a zero of 1/ f .

annotations.label: Sum of multiplicities on extended plane

anchor.snippet: if a function is meromorphic on the whole complex plane plus the point at infinity , then the sum of the multiplicities of its poles equals the sum of the multiplicities of its zeros
A function of a complex variable z is holomorphic in an open domain U if it is differentiable with respect to z at every point of U .
A function is meromorphic in U if every point of U has a neighbourhood such that at least one of f and 1/ f is holomorphic in it.

annotations.label: Zero and pole of a meromorphic function

anchor.snippet: A zero of a meromorphic function f is a complex number z such that f ( z ) = 0 . A pole of f is a zero of 1/ f .

annotations.label: Order of a pole and zero

anchor.snippet: If n > 0 , then
This characterization of zeros and poles implies that zeros and poles are isolated , that is, every zero or pole has a neighbourhood that does not contain any other zero and pole.
it is often useful to consider a pole of order n as a zero of order − n and a zero of order n as a pole of order − n

annotations.label: Gamma and zeta as meromorphic examples

anchor.snippet: This is the case for the gamma function (see the image in the infobox), which is meromorphic in the whole complex plane, and has a simple pole at every non-positive integer.
a nonzero meromorphic function f is the sum of a Laurent series with at most finite principal part

a polynomial of degree n has a pole of degree n at infinity
The complex plane extended by a point at infinity is called the Riemann sphere .

annotations.label: Sum of orders on Riemann sphere

anchor.snippet: If f is a function that is meromorphic on the whole Riemann sphere, then it has a finite number of zeros and poles, and the sum of the orders of its poles equals the sum of the orders of its zeros.

Every rational function is meromorphic on the whole Riemann sphere, and, in this case, the sum of orders of the zeros or of the poles is the maximum of the degrees of the numerator and the denominator.

annotations.label: Zeros and poles on a complex curve

anchor.snippet: The concept of zeros and poles extends naturally to functions on a complex curve , that is complex analytic manifold of dimension one (over the complex numbers).

annotations.label: Holomorphic/meromorphic on a curve via charts

annotations.label: Sum of orders on compact curve

anchor.snippet: If the curve is compact , and the function f is meromorphic on the whole curve, then the number of zeros and poles is finite, and the sum of the orders of the poles equals the sum of the orders of the zeros.
